rulings.Case Title: DANIEL MYTELS VS. AISAR ELVIN KHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 1, (2 of 2) Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration) Moreover, any uncertainty in interpreting the parties' agreement is to be resolved in favor of binding arbitration. In addition, when Mr. Mytels demanded arbitration pursuant to the binding rules of JAMS, Mr. Khan requested that the matter go to arbitration, which further shows that the parties contemplated binding and final arbitration. Mr. Khan fails to establish that Mr. Mytels needed to file his petition to confirm in federal court. 9 USC 9 provides that "If no court is specified in the agreement of the parties, then such application may be made to the United States court in and for the district within which such award was made." (emphasis added.) That code section does not require a federal forum. Furthermore, the agreement provides that "[a]rbitration shall be pursuant to the rules of California law." This provision establishes that it is proper for Mr. Mytels to seek confirmation in a California state court per the California Arbitration Act. Mr. Khan's cross-petition to vacate the arbitration award is denied for the same reasons why Mr. Mytels' petition to confirm is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Mytels is required to prepare a proposed statement of decision which conforms to this tentative ruling and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(end 2 of 2) =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL MYTELS VS. AISAR ELVIN KHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 1, (1 of 2) PETITIONER DANIEL MYTELS' Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration) Petitioner Daniel Mytels' petition to confirm contractual arbitration award is granted. There is no basis to vacate the award under CCP 1286.2. To the extent that respondent Aisar Khan contends that the arbitrator made legal and factual errors, California law does not allow for the vacating or correcting of an arbitration award based on errors of law or fact committed by an arbitrator. (Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal. 4th 1.) The court has assumed that the amendment to Mr. Khan's petition to vacate is timely and relates back to his original petition and addresses the merits of all of his arguments. The Independent Contractor agreement included an arbitration clause and the Joint Defense Agreement did not supersede it. There was no novation. Alexander v. Angel (1951) 37 Cal.2d 856, 862 states that "[w]here it 'satisfactorily appears that a new agreement was intended by the parties to take the place of an existing one,'" a novation exists. There is no language in the Joint Defense Agreement showing that the parties intended to displace their agreement to arbitrate. The record belies Mr. Khan's assertion that Mr. Mytels misled the arbitrator regarding arbitration as she expressly rejected Mr. Khan's position regarding the Joint Defense Agreement and its effect on arbitrability. Mr. Khan's argument that Mr. Mytels needed to assert his claims against Mr. Khan in the underlying Quadri action lacks merit. Mr. Mytels' claim against Mr. Khan for indemnity was not a compulsory cross-claim that was waived by not filing it. A compulsory cross-claim is a claim that needs to be asserted against the plaintiff, not against a co-defendant like Mr. Khan. (CCP 426.30.) Moreover, the dispute over the indemnity agreement does not arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the main claim and thus does not constitute a "related cause of action" within the meaning of CCP 426.10(c). There is nothing in the parties' arbitration agreement that suggests that it was not final and binding. Per California law, the term "arbitration" connotes binding arbitration absent express language to the contrary or a context (e.g. attorney-fee disputes and court-required arbitration) not applicable here. The reference to 9 USC 1 et seq., which governs resolution of binding arbitration agreements, strongly suggests that the arbitration was supposed to be final and binding. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO POLICE OFFICERS' ASSOCIATION VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 2, RESPONDENT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION Respondent City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the sole cause of action alleged by the three Retired Officers in their petition for writ of mandate for violation of Government Code 3304(b) is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. If they can do so in good faith, the Retired Officers have leave to allege specific facts that the administrative appeal afforded by the Police Commission was futile and did not comport with due process and/or the requirements of section 3304(b). For purposes of this demurrer, the court assumes that the Chief's decision to file a disciplinary complaint against the retired officers is "punitive action" within the meaning of section 3304(b). The petitioner and the judicially noticeable facts show that the Police Commission provides a comprehensive quasi-judicial hearing process to determine whether the Chief's complaint has merit and whether an officer should be disciplined. That detailed process presumptively comports with due process and section 3304(b). The fact that the Police Commission's process during the timeframe applicable to Retired Officers erroneously did not allow for a writ to be taken to the superior court after the Commission issued its decision by itself does not render the process fatally defective in these particular circumstances because Retired Officers retired before any sort of hearing occurred. It is quite possible that the Commission could have rejected the Chief's disciplinary complaints. Moreover, the Retired Officers could have sought pre-hearing relief to vindicate their POBRA rights with regard to the lack of writ relief or other perceived problems with the Police Commission's hearing process. (Alameida v. State Personnel Bd. (2004) 120 Cal.App.4th 46, 54; Mounger v. Gates (1987) 193 Cal.App.3d 1248, 1256-1257.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for CCSF is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: JANET M. PLANELLS VS. STATE OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice And Motion Of Defendant Healthright 360 To Compel Plaintiff To Respond To Special Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT HEALTHRIGHT 360, A NONPROFIT CORPORATION'S Motion Of Defendant Healthright 360 To Compel Plaintiff To Respond To Special Interrogatories Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant HealthRight 360's unopposed motion to compel responses to its first set of interrogatories is granted. Plaintiff failed to serve a timely response to the special interrogatories and has therefore waived any objection to the interrogatories, including one based on privilege or on the protection of work product, subject to the provisions for relief from such waiver set forth in CCP § 2030.290(a). Plaintiff is to provide code-compliant responses within 14 days of the entry of this order. Such responses shall be without objections unless a motion for relief from waiver has been filed. Defendant has not requested discovery sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross Defendant Sun Roofing & Construction Co., Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 4, Cross Defendant Sun Roofing & Construction Co., Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment Cross-defendant's Sun Roofing & Construction Co., Inc.'s motion for summary judgment on the MBC Parties' cross-complaint is granted. Per the October 2, 2018 order, the only remaining causes of action in the MBC Parties' cross-complaint alleged against Sun Roofing are the first, fourth and fifth causes of action for express contractual indemnity, breach of contract, and breach of warranty. As to the cause of action for express contractual indemnity, the MBC Parties have not produced evidence that the contract faxed to Sun Roofing, signed by Sun Roofing and returned for signature to MBC and signed by MBC contained the pages with the General Subcontractor Provisions. The evidence that MBC has a business practice of mailing the contract documents as well, while sending a fax, does not create a triable dispute whether at the time Sun Roofing signed the contract, Sun Roofing was clearly directed where to find the provisions or that they were known or easily available to it, therefore there is no valid incorporation by reference of the indemnity provisions into the MBC -Sun Roofing subcontract. (Perez v. DirecTV Group Holdings, LLC (C.D. Cal. 2017) 251 F.Supp.3d 1328, 1339). MBC cannot show damages for its breach of contract and breach of warranty claims because the good faith settlement determination precludes plaintiff from seeking liability from MBC for any damages caused by Sun Roofing's work on the project and MBC through these causes of action seeks to impose liability on Sun Roofing based on Sun Roofing's share of fault to plaintiff. There is no evidence of a contractual relation between the developer entities and Sun Roofing and MBC has no express contractual indemnity liability to the developer entities. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Sun Roofing is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT BROADWAY MECHANICAL-CONTRACTORS, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or Alternatively, For Summary Adjudication Cross-defendant's Broadway Mechanical Contractors, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment on the MBC Parties' cross-complaint is granted. The motion is not premature because the MBC Parties have not shown that any new facts alleged in plaintiff's third amended complaint would provide any new legal or factual bases on which the MBC Parties would seek relief from Broadway or that Broadway's burden on this motion will be materially affected unlike in Perry v. Atkinson (1987) 195 Cal.App.3d 14. The settlement agreement provides that the contracts delineate Broadway's scope of work on the project and release Broadway and the MBC Parties from liability arising from this work. Broadway has submitted evidence that Green Again was a separate corporate entity in which Broadway's owner had a property interest, the two entities had no common operations at the relevant time, and Green Again signed a separate subcontract with an indemnity clause with MBC. The MBC Parties have not presented any facts creating a triable dispute as to Broadway's liability for Green Again's work based on an alter ego or single enterprise theory because there is no evidence of an unequitable result if separate corporate structure is maintained. (Toho-Towa Co., Ltd. v. Morgan Creek Productions, Inc. (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 1096, 1108 ("In California, common principles apply regardless of whether the alleged alter ego is based on piercing the corporate veil to attach liability to a shareholder or to hold a corporation liable as part of a single enterprise.")). There is also no evidence of Broadway's authority to control Green Again based on which a reasonable trier of fact could find Broadway vicariously liable on an agency theory. MBC Parties' claims for equitable indemnity against Broadway are barred by the good faith settlement determination. MBC Parties' claims for breach of warranty, breach of contract and negligence are also barred because they seek to impose liability on Broadway for its share of fault to plaintiff because the MBC Parties' entitlement to indemnity and contribution rests on their ability to prove the contract claims. By defending and settling claims against Broadway and MBC arising from Broadway's scope of work, Broadway fulfilled its express contractual indemnity and defense obligations towards MBC, and there is no evidence that Broadway has such express indemnity and duty to defend obligations towards the developer entities. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Broadway is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL

Appointment of a discovery referee over a party's objection requires "exceptional circumstances." (CCP 639(d)(2)). Even assuming that the great majority of defendants' 190 discovery requests were improper, that does not, without more, establish exceptional circumstances. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Plaintiff's counsel is reminded of the requirements of Local Rule 2.7B regarding the lodging of courtesy copies and the potential consequences of failing to do so. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: AIRCO MECHANICAL INC. VS. BUILD GROUP INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 11, CROSS DEFENDANT HARRY WINSTON, INC. (FROM CASE CGC-18-565880) DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT Cross-defendant Harry Winston, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended cross-complaint filed by E.J. Weber Electric Company, Inc. is sustained without leave to amend as to the eighth and ninth causes of action for intentional interference with contract and overruled as to the sixth tenth, eleventh and twelfth causes of action for negligence, declaratory relief, equitable indemnity and comparative indemnity. Because HWI (as the lessee and beneficiary of all the construction and improvement work) is a third beneficiary of the contracts it allegedly interfered with, HWI is not a stranger to those contracts and cannot be liable for interfering with those contracts. While the negligence claim has some attributes similar to a nonactionable claim for negligent interference with contract, liberally construed the negligence claim is based on a legal duty separate from a duty not to interfere with Weber Electric's contracts. Weber Electric has adequately alleged a legal duty sounding in negligence on the part of HWI not to take any action or refrain from taking any action that would cause foreseeable harm to Weber Electric. The declaratory relief claim is adequately alleged since there is a current dispute regarding the responsibility for the damages claimed by Build Group, Inc. The negligence claim is a sufficient predicate for the indemnity claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Weber Electric is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL MOORE VS. PILOT AI LABS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANT NEW ENTERPRISE ASSOCIATES 15, L.P.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant New Enterprise Associates 15, L.P.'s demurrer to all twelve causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Rachel Moore is sustained without leave to amend as to all twelve causes of action. Ms. Moore has not alleged, nor does it appear that she can allege, sufficient ultimate facts to show that the alleged misconduct of Rick Yang is attributable to NEA. The factual allegations of the complaint (as opposed to the conclusions of law therein) show that at all times Mr. Yang participated in any of the conduct for which Ms. Moore seeks to hold NEA liable, Mr. Yang was acting in his capacity as a board member of defendant Pilot AI, Inc., not as an agent of NEA. Because NEA possesses no legal right to control Pilot AI's board member activities, as a matter of law Mr. Yang was not acting as NEA's agent while he oversaw the investigation into Ms. Moore's harassment complaint. Furthermore, NEA is not liable based on respondeat superior because sexual harassment is not an "inevitable risk" flowing from NEA's venture capital enterprise. Regarding FEHA aiding and abetting, Gibson-Jones v. Berkel & Company Contractors (N.D. Cal. 2008) 2008 WL 782568 is distinguishable because that case only stands for the proposition that an aiding and abetting theory may apply to an investigator who allegedly engaged in abuses. Gibson-Jones does not state or suggest that aiding and abetting liability may be extended to the employer of a board member who oversaw such an investigation. The joint employer theory is inapplicable because, even with added information about NEA's involvement in recruitment and some trainings, Ms. Moore cannot allege sufficient facts that NEA had control over her day-to-day employment activities. The integrated enterprise theory is likewise inapplicable because that theory only applies to a parent company and subsidiary, not to an investor and a portfolio company. Finally, because FEHA ratification can only be done by an employer in response to actions of its employees and because NEA is not the employer of defendants Jonathan Su and Robert English, NEA cannot have ratified their alleged misconduct. Since there is no indication that Ms. Moore can amend her complaint to allege a viable claim against NEA, leave to amend is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for NEA is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: HIRAM BORUNDA VS. MAKARIZO, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Proposed Order; And Declaration Of James F. Peterson Regarding Compliance With Meet And Confer Requirements Of Code Of Civil Procedure 435.5 (A)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, November 13, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT BROOKS BROTHERS GROUP, INC.'s Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Proposed Order; And Declaration Of James F. Peterson Regarding Compliance With Meet And Confer Requirements Of Code Of Civil Procedure 435.5 (A) Defendant Brooks Brothers Group, Inc.'s motion to strike portions of the complaint is granted as to all references to death and wrongful death and denied as to the allegations and prayer for punitive damages. Plaintiff Hiram Borunda acknowledges that the references to death and wrongful death should be stricken. Liberally construed, the complaint alleges that Brooks Brothers: 1) knowingly sold a dangerous and defective shirt due to its lack of needed fire retardant and fire resistant features; 2) knew that the shirt could easily catch and spread a fire causing serious injuries to any person who wore the shirt or had it in his possession; and 3) knowingly failed to warn of these issues and to take any other measures to protect the safety of persons who wore the shirt or had in their possessions. These allegations are sufficient to support punitive damages liability based on despicable conduct carried on with a willful and conscious disregard for the safety of others. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Borunda is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
